coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescent carbon dots synthesized by microwave treatment for selective image of cancer cells

coredata.dc:description: In this work, a simple, low-cost and one-step microwave approach has been demonstrated for the synthesis of water-soluble carbon dots (C-dots). The average size of the resulting C-dots is about 4nm. From the photoluminescence (PL) measurements, the C-dots exhibit excellent biocompatibility and intense PL with the high quantum yield (QY) at Ca. 25%. Significantly, the C-dots have excellent biocompatibility and the capacity to specifically target the cells overexpressing the folate receptor (FR). These exciting results indicate the as-prepared C-dots are promising biocompatible probe for cancer diagnosis and treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Selective image
Cancer cells detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the C-dots
Cancer cells detection using C-dots as the fluorescent probes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis procedure of luminescent C-dots from FA and urea via microwave carbonization approach.
(a) TEM image of synthesized C-dots, scale bar: 25nm; (b) high-resolution TEM image of C-dots, scale bar: 5nm; (c) the corresponding particle size distribution histograms.
(a) XRD pattern and (b) XPS of C-dots; the inset is high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s of C-dots.

(a) UV–vis absorption (i), PL excitation (ii) and emission (iii) spectra of C-dots in aqueous solution (λ ex =380nm). Insets show photographs of the C-dots in aqueous solution under visible (iv) and UV (v) light. (b) PL emission spectra of C-dots in aqueous solution at different excitation wavelengths (from 300 to 460nm in 20nm increments).
Viability of GES-1 cells (a) and HeLa cells (b) after 24, 48 and 72h of incubation with different concentrations of C-dots in the cell medium as determined by MTT assays.
Confocal images of GES-1 cells incubated with C-dots for 4h (a), 8h (b), and HeLa cells incubated with C-dots for 1h (c); (i) bright-field, (ii) confocal fluorescent and (iii) overlay images.

head.ce:title: Photoluminescent carbon dots synthesized by microwave treatment for selective image of cancer cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Changchun University of Technology, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The First Hospital of Jilin University, Changchun 130021, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The First Hospital of Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Lab of Polymer Composites Engineering, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun 130022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a simple, low-cost and one-step microwave approach has been demonstrated for the synthesis of water-soluble carbon dots (C-dots). The average size of the resulting C-dots is about 4nm. From the photoluminescence (PL) measurements, the C-dots exhibit excellent biocompatibility and intense PL with the high quantum yield (QY) at Ca. 25%. Significantly, the C-dots have excellent biocompatibility and the capacity to specifically target the cells overexpressing the folate receptor (FR). These exciting results indicate the as-prepared C-dots are promising biocompatible probe for cancer diagnosis and treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of cell labeling techniques has drawn much attention in recent years [1–2]. However, there still exists a challenging subject for detecting or tracing cancer cells from normal cells. In particular, targeted fluorescent materials for cancer cell imaging are highly desirable for early stage cancer diagnosis and treatment. Folate receptor (FR) is well known to be upregulated in a variety of human cancerous cells, including the malignancies of ovary, mammary gland, lung, kidney, brain, prostate and throat; while in other cells FR is only minimally distributed [3–6]. For FR, folic acid (FA) conjugated with fluorescent nanoparticles and molecules have been widely used for cellular imaging, and such technology has also been employed as an important strategies to distinguish the cancer cell from normal cells [3,6–9].

In the past years, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) have attracted much attention due to their potential applications in many fields [10–15]. However, most traditional QDs contain heavy metal elements, which are under-utilized due to their toxicity, low stability, environmental hazard and raise significant concerns regarding the potential toxicity of using them in biological systems [16–18]. Therefore, the search for benign nanomaterials with similar optical properties has become an urgent challenge [19–23].

Recently, carbon dots (C-dots), as new members of the quantum sized carbon analogues, have emerged as environmentally friendly complements to chalcogenide-based quantum dots [24–27]. Their excellent fluorescence, stabilization and low toxicity make them as attractive materials for lots of promising applications, such as bioimaging [28–31], sensors [27,29,32], photovoltaics [33], electronic and optoelectronic devices [34]. To date, various synthesis methods have been developed for the synthesis of C-dots, such as fragmentation/fractionation of suitable carbon source followed by surface passivation or alternatively by bottom-up strategies utilizing molecular precursors, and some other techniques such as combustion, thermal, or microwave treatments [35–44]. Owing to their photoluminescent feature, C-dots have been widely studied for optical bioimaging in vitro and in vivo [45–48]. However, to the best of our knowledge, selectivity is still challenging for application of C-dots in bioimaging [3,49–52]. Furthermore, no research has reported to synthesis C-dots adopting FA as carbon source for targeting and detecting cancer cells.

Herein, we report a simple, low-cost and one-step microwave route for the synthesis of photoluminescent water-soluble C-dots from FA and urea. The as-prepared C-dots exhibit intense photoluminescence (PL) with the high quantum yield (QY) at Ca. 25%. Moreover, because the FA molecules were introduced into the C-dots, the C-dots can be devoured preferentially by FA-receptor-positive cancer cells. As a result, the C-dots can be used as a new biocompatible probe to distinguish FA-receptor-positive cancer cells from normal cells. Our unprecedented PL C-dots are potential to be used in biological and chemical fields, including biological imaging, cancer diagnosis, and externally activated release of anticancer drugs.

Folic acid (FA) was purchased from Sigma–Aldrich. Urea was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). The ultra-pure water used throughout the experiment was purified with a Milli-Q A10 filtration system (Millipore, Billerica, MA, USA). All other reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

The morphology and mean diameter of C-dots were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F) and High-resolution TEM (HTEM, FEI TECNAI F20). AFM images were recorded in the tapping mode with a Nanoscope IIIa scanning probe microscope from Digital Instruments under ambient conditions (cantilever: NCHR-50, S/N76842F2L991). A droplet of C-dots dispersion was cast onto a freshly cleaved mica surface, followed by drying at room temperature. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained by using a Lambad 800 UV–vis spectrophotometer. FT-IR spectra were taken on a Nicolet Avatar 360 FT-IR spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were performed with a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC spectrophotometer. XRD data were collected on a Siemens D-5005 X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ =1.5418 Å). The confocal microscopy images were taken with a Olympus Fluoview FV1000. All measurements were performed at room temperature under ambient conditions.

The C-dots were prepared as follows: Firstly, 2g FA and 2g urea were dissolved into 10mL distilled water to form a well-distributed solution. Then the solution was put into a domestic 500W microwave oven for 8min. During this process, the solution changed from yellow to brown and finally dark-brown clustered solid, which indicated the formation of C-dots. Then the solid was naturally cooled to room temperature and dissolved in DI water, forming the C-dots solution. The solution was centrifuged at 3000rpm for 10min to remove small volumes of liquid and filtered through a 0.22μm membrane to remove large or agglomerated particles. To further purify the C-dots, they were added to water and subjected to dialysis against pure water through a membrane (MWCO=3.5–5 KD, Float-A-Lyzer G2, Spectrum Laboratories, Rancho Dominguez, CA, USA) for 3h. Finally, the C-dots of clear, light, yellow–brown aqueous were obtained.

The QY of C-dots was calculated by comparing their integrated PL intensities (excitation at 365nm) and absorbance values at 365nm with those of quinine sulfate. Quinine sulfate (QY=0.54) was dissolved in 0.1M H2SO4 (refractive index: 1.33) and the C-dots were dissolved in water (refractive index: 1.33). In order to minimize reabsorption effects, absorbance value in the 10mm fluorescence cuvette was kept below 0.10 at the excitation wavelength. Excitation and emission slit widths were set at 5.0nm when recording their PL spectra. The QY was calculated by the following equation:
ø
x
=
ø
st
(
I
x
/
I
st
) (
η
x
2

η
st
2
) (
A
st

A
x
)
where ø is the QY, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and A is the optical density. The subscript “st” refers to standard with know QY and “x” for the sample.

GES-1 and HeLa cells were cultured in high-glucose Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) media supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin/streptomycin at 37°C and 5% CO2, respectively. Suspensions (20μg/mL) of C-dots from the stock solution were prepared with serum-containing DMEM. 10×104 cells were seeded in a 6-well cell culture plate in cell culture medium. On the treatment day, the culture medium was replaced with 100 μL of the medium containing 20 μL/mL C-dots. The cells were then incubated at 37°C for 30min, 1h, 2h, 4h, 8h, and 12h, respectively. The excess C-dots were removed by washing 3 times with warm phosphate buffered saline (PBS).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental procedure of this work is depicted in Fig. 1 . FA and urea were added to distilled water to form a well-distributed solution. The solution was then heated in a domestic 500W microwave oven for 8min. The reaction was conducted by first condensing FA and urea, whereupon they formed polymer-like C-dots, which were then carbonized to form the C-dots. During this process, the carbogenic nanoparticles were passivated by urea, the solution changed from yellow to brown and finally dark-brown clustered solid, which indicated the formation of C-dots. The corresponding reaction was conducted by first condensing FA and urea under the microwave to form polymer-like C-dots, which were then carbonized to form the C-dots (Fig. S1).

The morphology of the C-dots is characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). The TEM image (Fig. 2 a) illustrates that the C-dots are well-dispersed nanoparticles with very small size. From the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), most particles are observed to be amorphous carbon particles without any lattices (Fig. 2b). According to TEM measurement, the statistical particle size distribution of C-dots is mainly in the range of 1–7nm in diameter (Fig. 2c). AFM is also used to observe the C-dots, the AFM image also supports the spherical morphology. The average heights is about 3.2nm, which matches well with the TEM results (Fig. S2).

As shown in Fig. 3 a, a typical X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern express a board peak located at 27.4°, which also indicates the highly disordered carbon atoms [52]. The surface groups are also investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis (Fig. 3b). As shown in the inset of Fig. 3b, C1s analysis reveal three different types of carbon atoms: CC/CC, CN/CN and CO(O) with their contents 50.08%, 23.2% and 26.72%, respectively. 13C NMR spectroscopy is an effective technique to distinguish sp3-hybridized carbon atoms from sp2-hybridized carbon atoms. The existence of sp2- and sp3-hybridized carbon atoms in as-prepared C-dots are confirmed from the 13C NMR spectrum of the C-dots in D2O (Fig. S3). In the 13C NMR spectrum, signals in the ranges of 30–45ppm correspond to aliphatic (sp3-hybridized) carbon atoms, and signals from 110 to 185ppm, ppm are assigned to sp2-hybridized carbon atoms. The signals in the range of 170–180ppm indicate the existence of carboxyl/amide groups [29,53]. Moreover, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra was acquired to determine their surface state, the C-dots displays many common characteristics in the spectra. Contrast with free FA molecules, the C-dots mainly retained the molecular structures of FA (Fig. S4). Broad absorption bands at 3100–3500cm− 1 are assigned to ν(OH) and ν(NH). Absorption bands of 1600–1740cm− 1, and 1350–1450cm− 1 are assigned to ν(CO) and δ(CH2), respectively [29,39].

Fig. 4 a depicts the UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of C-dots. The aqueous solution of C-dots has a broad absorption spectrum with peak at 350nm. In the PL spectra, the C-dots in aqueous solution have excitation and emission wavelengths at 380nm and 460nm, respectively. They exhibit light brown color under ambient light, and show blue color under a hand-held UV lamp. The aqueous solution of C-dots exhibits excitation-independent PL behaviors (Fig. 4b). When the excitation wavelength is changed from 300 to 460nm, the corresponding PL emission peaks for C-dots do not show great shifts. This behavior is the result of the surface state/molecular state and particle size distribution of the C-dots [29]. Furthermore, the strongest PL emission band, located at 460nm, is obtained under 380nm excitation and has a high fluorescent quantum yield of 25% (using quinine sulfate as a reference). The PL stabilities of C-dots under the effects of the ions strength, pH and UV exposure were also investigated (Fig. S5). There are no changes in PL intensity and peak position at different ionic strengths. The PL intensities of the C-dots keep constant when the pH of the solution between 3 and 11, but decrease when the pH beyond this range. The photobleaching was occurred after 5h of continuous UV excitation. These results may be a consequence of the chemical structure changes of C-dots. Most of the C-dots have no graphite lattices, the emission control of the surface state/molecule state is affected by extreme conditions, such as too low/high pH or high-power UV radiation [29]. However, these results of PL stability suggest that the C-dots are suitable for bioimaging application.

To achieve imaging in cells, the as-prepared C-dots need to exhibit good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. The MTT (3-(4,5)-dimethylthiahiazol(-z-y1)-3,5-di-phenytetrazoliumromide) assays and apoptosis assay were used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of C-dots and the viability of cells. From the results of the MTT assay (Fig. 5 ), the viability of GES-1 and HeLa cells remained above 90% after incubation with C-dots even at the concentration of 100mg/L for 72h. These performances imply that our C-dots can simplify discrimination of FR-positive cancerous cells from normal cells. As a result, the as-prepared C-dots are suitable labeling probe for imaging in cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cancer cells detection using C-dots as the fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: The application of C-dots to distinguish cancer cells from normal cells was evaluated by culturing and analyzing model cells of GES-1 and HeLa cells. GES-1 and HeLa cells are selected as normal and cancerous cells, respectively. As demonstrated in Fig. 6 , there is no fluorescence signal in the GES-1 cells incubated with C-dots for 4h while the GES-1 cells incubated with C-dots for 8h and the HeLa cells incubated with C-dots for 1h exhibit bright fluorescence. Compared with normal GES-1 cells, the HeLa cells show sensitive endocytosis of the C-dots in a short time. These results are probably due to the specific interactions between FA of C-dots and folate receptors in the HeLa cancerous cell membrance, which should favor folate receptor mediated endocytosis. These performances imply that our C-dots can simply discrimination of FR-positive cancerous cells from normal cells.

In summary, we report a facile method to prepare the water-soluble photoluminescent C-dots using FA and urea under the microwave condition. The method is relatively simple, economic, and environmentally friendly. The as-obtained C-dots show high quantum yield, excellent ability and good biocompatibility. Moreover, the C-dots can be directly applied in the imaging of cells without further functionalization and show specific targeting ability to cancer cells that overexpress the FA receptor. Therefore, the C-dots with the facile synthesis method can be a promising material for cancer diagnosis and treatment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21174048, 51373061 and 21304090), the National Basic Research Program of China (2012CB933802), and the “111” project (B06009).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2015.06.002.
